file_name: Nikitaev et al. - 2024 - The method for evaluating the symmetry of the globule pattern in artificial intelligence systems for.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used the Random Forest machine learning algorithm to classify dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms based on 12 quantitative symmetry characteristics of globular patterns, achieving 85% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the development of a new method for evaluating symmetry, the formation of a specific dataset of 100 dermoscopic images, the implementation of a Random Forest classifier, and the reporting of experimental results (85% accuracy). It is not a review or survey of prior literature.

title: The method for evaluating the symmetry of the globule pattern in artificial intelligence systems for the diagnosis of skin neoplasms
brief_summary: This study addresses the lack of interpretability in neural network-based melanoma diagnosis by proposing a method for evaluating the symmetry of globular patterns in dermoscopic images. The researchers developed a dataset of 100 dermoscopic images, comprising 50 cases with symmetric and 50 with asymmetric globular distributions, verified by expert dermatologists. The method involves automatic segmentation of the neoplasm area and globules, followed by the calculation of 12 quantitative symmetry characteristics regarding coordinates and colors. A Random Forest classifier was trained on these features to distinguish between symmetric and asymmetric patterns. The experimental results demonstrated a classification accuracy of 85% using five-fold cross-validation. The authors conclude that this interpretable approach enhances diagnostic transparency and supports clinical decision-making for skin neoplasm diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on a dataset of 100 dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms, specifically categorized by the symmetry of their globular patterns (symmetric vs. asymmetric). While melanoma is mentioned as the primary clinical motivation for early diagnosis, the specific experimental dataset and classification task were based on the morphological feature of globule symmetry within skin neoplasms rather than distinct histological diagnoses like melanoma vs. nevus.

Melanoma is cited as the most aggressive form of skin cancer and the primary motivation for developing early non-invasive diagnostic methods. It is mentioned in the introduction and conclusion as the target disease for screening, but the specific 100-image dataset used for the symmetry evaluation experiment was not explicitly labeled as containing only melanoma or specific histological classes, but rather 'skin neoplasms' grouped by globule symmetry.

Mentioned in the context of differential diagnosis against melanoma. The study aims to improve differential diagnosis between melanoma and benign skin neoplasms, but the specific experimental dataset was defined by globule symmetry rather than benign/malignant histology.

all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task where a Random Forest classifier predicts whether a dermoscopic image of a skin neoplasm has a symmetric or asymmetric globular pattern. The dataset consisted of 100 images (50 symmetric, 50 asymmetric), and the model achieved 85% accuracy using five-fold cross-validation.

datasets_used.name: Dermatology Clinic Research Center Dataset (Moscow, Russia)
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset of dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms collected from the 'Dermatology Clinic' Research Center in Moscow, Russia. The original pool contained 656 images, from which a subset of 100 images was selected for the study based on criteria including clearly defined globular patterns, image quality, and absence of artifacts. The images were captured using an RDS-2 dermatoscope (2560×1920 pixels, 24-bit color depth).

use.training: Used to train a Random Forest classifier for distinguishing between symmetric and asymmetric globular patterns based on 12 quantitative symmetry characteristics.
use.validation: Used for five-fold cross-validation to assess the effectiveness of the proposed method and obtain statistically significant results.
use.testing: Used for five-fold cross-validation to assess the effectiveness of the proposed method and obtain statistically significant results.
use.other: The selection was verified by expert dermatologists to serve as a reference standard.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess the effectiveness of the proposed method and obtain statistically significant results for the classification accuracy.
To tune the hyperparameters of the Random Forest classifier (number of trees, maximum depth, minimum samples in a leaf).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It mentions that the sample size was sufficient for obtaining 'statistically significant results during cross-validation' and that Random Forest demonstrated the 'best results' compared to SVM and logistic regression, but no specific statistical tests or alpha thresholds were provided.

Dermatology Clinic Research Center Datas.description: Custom dataset of 100 dermoscopic images of skin neoplasms (50 symmetric, 50 asymmetric globular patterns). Evaluated using five-fold cross-validation.
Dermatology Clinic Research Center Datas.notes: Random Forest demonstrated the best results among the tested algorithms. The dataset was divided into five parts for cross-validation, with four parts used for training and one for testing in each fold. Final evaluations were calculated as averages across all five folds.

architecture_used.input_size: 2560x1920 pixels (original images); 12-dimensional feature vector (input to classifier)

architecture_details.algorithm: Random Forest ensemble of decision trees

feature_extraction.method: Manual calculation of 12 quantitative symmetry characteristics
features: Ratios of mean coordinates of globules in different quadrants (k1x, k1y)
Ratios of mean color components (red, green, blue) of globules in different quadrants (k2x_red, k2x_green, k2x_blue, k2y_red, k2y_blue, k2y_green)
Ratios of standard deviations of coordinates in different quadrants (k3yx>0, k3xx>0, k3xy>0, k3yy>0)

architecture_details.segmentation_method: Threshold filtering in HSV color space (Hue channel) with binarization threshold 0.2

classification_or_output_head.output: Class label based on majority voting of decision trees

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel
preprocessing_techniques: Selection of images based on quality criteria: clearly defined globular patterns, image quality, and absence of artifacts.
Conversion of images from RGB to HSV color space for robust segmentation against lighting changes.
Threshold filtering in the Hue channel of HSV color space with a binarization threshold of 0.2 to segment the neoplasm area.
Calculation of the neoplasm center coordinates as the average of all non-zero pixels in the binary mask.
Application of the Canny edge detector for globule detection.
Area- and perimeter-based filtering to exclude structural objects differing from globules.
Calculation of globule centers as the center of the inscribed circle within the globule contour.
conclusions: The study developed an interpretable method for evaluating the symmetry of globular patterns in dermoscopic images to address the lack of transparency in neural network-based melanoma diagnosis.
A custom dataset of 100 dermoscopic images (50 symmetric, 50 asymmetric) was created and verified by expert dermatologists.
The method utilizes automatic segmentation of the neoplasm area and globules, followed by the calculation of 12 quantitative symmetry characteristics regarding coordinates and colors.

A Random Forest classifier trained on these 12 features achieved a classification accuracy of 85% using five-fold cross-validation, outperforming Support Vector Machine (82%) and Logistic Regression (78%).
The proposed approach is generalizable and can be applied to assess the symmetry of any structural elements of skin neoplasms, not just globules.
The method enhances diagnostic transparency and supports clinical decision-making, particularly in differential diagnosis between melanoma and benign skin neoplasms.
The developed software module can be integrated into computer vision systems for dermatoscopy, telemedicine, and mobile applications for melanoma screening.
The authors acknowledge that further research is needed for clinical validation and assessment of the method's impact on diagnostic quality in real-world practice.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-center dataset of only 100 dermoscopic images (50 symmetric, 50 asymmetric) sourced from one clinic in Moscow, Russia, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population bias.

The ground truth labels for symmetry were determined by expert dermatologist consensus rather than histopathological diagnosis, meaning the model predicts morphological symmetry rather than malignancy directly.

The dataset was curated based on specific inclusion criteria (clearly defined globular patterns, high quality, no artifacts), which may introduce selection bias and limit applicability to real-world clinical images with poor quality or complex patterns.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely using five-fold cross-validation on the internal dataset, which may overestimate performance.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) to support the claim of 'statistically significant results,' relying instead on the assertion that the sample size was sufficient.

The segmentation and feature extraction methods (HSV thresholding, Canny edge detection) are traditional computer vision techniques that may lack robustness compared to deep learning approaches when handling varied lighting conditions or complex lesion boundaries.

The primary clinical relevance is limited to the assessment of globular symmetry as a feature; the study does not demonstrate the impact of this feature on the final diagnostic accuracy for melanoma vs. benign lesions in a clinical workflow.
The authors acknowledge the need for further clinical validation and assessment of the method's impact on diagnostic quality in real-world practice, indicating the current evidence is preliminary.
